term: The Ultimate Push Pull Legs System
definition: A 5x/week hypertrophy-first push/pull/legs training system organized in a precise, calculated way and built around three sequenced phases.
related_terms: hypertrophy, periodization, Base Phase, Maximum Overload Phase, Supercompensation Phase

definition: The growth of muscle tissue and the primary adaptation target of this program.
related_terms: volume, hard set, progressive overload, effort

definition: The ability to produce force. In this program it is supported and maintained, but not prioritized equally with hypertrophy.
Phase 1 of the program, using moderate-high volume and moderate-high intensity to lay the foundation for hypertrophy.
Phase 2 of the program, using low volume and ultra-high intensity to increase familiarity with true muscular failure and ensure progressive overload is occurring.
related_terms: progressive overload, muscular failure, intensity, low volume

definition: Phase 3 of the program, using ultra-high volume and moderate intensity to create a large new growth stimulus and rapidly increase muscle size.
The organization of training over time. In this document it is expressed through the program’s three-phase structure.
The gradual increase of stress placed on the body during training, usually through more weight, more reps, or improved execution and stimulus quality over time.
The total amount of work performed, approximated in the document mainly as tough working sets.
Effort and load. The document uses intensity to describe how demanding the work is, not just percentage of 1RM.
How hard a set is pushed relative to failure, measured using ideas like RPE and proximity to failure.
related_terms: RPE, hard set, muscular failure, intensity

definition: How often a given muscle or lift is directly trained every seven days.
The weight of the external resistance being used.
A working set taken within 0 to 3 reps of failure rather than a warm-up set.
Rate of perceived exertion, a 1 to 10 scale estimating how close a set was to failure. A 10 means failure was reached, 9 means one more rep was possible, and 8 means two more reps were possible.
related_terms: effort, hard set, LSRPE, muscular failure

definition: Last set RPE, shorthand for the RPE recorded on the final set of an exercise.
Range of motion.
The speed at which the lift occurs.
The contracting or positive phase of a lift.
The lowering or negative phase of a lift.
Delayed onset muscle soreness.
Abbreviation for dumbbell.
Reps emphasizing the stretched or bottom portion of a lift, used as an advanced hypertrophy technique.
Preparatory sets used to get ready for harder work sets without being the main work themselves.
A set-extension method where technique or leverage is changed mid-set instead of reducing load.
Controlled, exercise-specific use of body English or looser form to extend or overload a set on selected movements.
Repeated low-rep mini-sets with very short rest, often done as many small sets rather than one traditional long set.
The more fixed muscle attachment point that does not move during contraction.
The muscle attachment point that moves more when the muscle contracts.
An anatomy approach focused on what muscles actually do so exercise selection can be matched to muscle function.
The movement-based understanding of how muscles and joints function, used here to guide smarter training decisions.
A four-muscle group on the front of the thigh whose main action is knee extension.
related_terms: vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, rectus femoris, vastus intermedius, knee extension

definition: One of the quadriceps muscles, referred to in the document as the 'quad sweep.'
One of the quadriceps muscles, referred to in the document as the 'tear drop.'
One of the quadriceps muscles and the more central visible upper-thigh portion described in the document.
One of the quadriceps muscles that lies underneath the rectus femoris.
Straightening the knee from a bent position to a straight position.
A posterior thigh muscle complex that collectively flexes the knee and extends the hip.
related_terms: semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris, knee flexion, hip extension

definition: One of the hamstring muscles involved in knee flexion and hip extension.
One of the hamstring muscles involved in knee flexion and hip extension.
A hamstring muscle with a long head and short head, contributing to knee flexion and hip extension roles.
related_terms: hamstrings, long head, short head, knee flexion

definition: Bending the knee from a straightened position to a bent position.
Driving the hips forward or moving the thigh backward, as in deadlift-type patterns.
A hip muscle complex including gluteus maximus, gluteus medius, and gluteus minimus, involved in both force production and stability.
related_terms: gluteus maximus, gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, hip abduction, hip extension

definition: The largest gluteal muscle and a major contributor to hip extension, hip abduction, hip external rotation, and posterior pelvic tilt.
related_terms: gluteals, hip extension, hip external rotation

definition: A gluteal muscle emphasized in the document for dynamic stability and hip abduction.
The smallest of the three gluteal muscles.
Moving the thigh away from the body’s midline.
related_terms: gluteus medius, gluteals, machine hip abduction

definition: Rotating the thigh outward at the hip.
Tucking the pelvis or butt inward, listed as one of the glute max functions.
The chest muscles, mainly pectoralis major and pectoralis minor, responsible for bringing the arm across the body and internally rotating the shoulder.
related_terms: pectoralis major, pectoralis minor, shoulder adduction, internal rotation

definition: The main chest muscle, divided into clavicular and sternal regions in the document.
The smaller pectoral muscle lying underneath pectoralis major.
The upper-chest portion of the pectoralis major associated with the clavicle and shoulder flexion assistance.
The lower-chest portion of the pectoralis major associated with the sternum.
Bringing the upper arm toward or across the body, one of the primary actions of the pecs and lats in different contexts.
Rotating the upper arm inward at the shoulder joint.
A large back muscle running from under the armpit toward the lower back, mainly acting to extend and adduct the shoulder.
A large upper-back muscle spanning from the skull base down toward the middle back and involved in scapular elevation and retraction.
Pulling the upper arm downward or backward, a key action of the lats.
Lifting the shoulder blades upward, one of the listed actions of the trapezius.
Pulling the shoulder blades backward toward the spine, one of the listed actions of the trapezius.
A two-headed upper-arm muscle involved in elbow flexion and forearm supination.
related_terms: long head, short head, brachialis, supination

definition: A strong elbow flexor that runs underneath the biceps brachii.
Bending the elbow from a straightened position to a bent position.
Twisting the forearm so the pinky/upward orientation described in the document is achieved, listed as a major biceps function.
A three-headed upper-arm muscle group whose collective main job is elbow extension.
related_terms: long head, medial head, lateral head, elbow extension

definition: Straightening the elbow from a bent position to a straightened position.
Sets performed after a heavier top set, used here as part of the program’s minimalistic strength support structure.
The point where no further reps can be completed with the required standard, treated in the document as the anchor for RPE 10 and a major concept in Phase 2.
